findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 12, Slide 14, Slide 18

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) + ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset + ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 + ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've nailed the market opportunity, but investors will want to hear directly from the people who actually experience these problems — add a slide showing 3-4 customer discovery highlights, like "Interviewed 12 villa developers in Dubai; 10 cited corrosion and maintenance as top barrier to solar adoption" or "Received LOI from [Developer Name] for 50-unit pilot pending reliability improvements." This transforms your mandate-and-problems story from logical to urgent.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they feel like a checklist rather than a timeline—add one concrete regulatory deadline or enforcement date that creates urgency in the next 12-24 months (for example, "Dubai's Estidama Phase 3 enforcement in Q3 2025 will require solar hot water on 80% of new villa permits, creating a compliance crisis for developers currently using unreliable legacy systems"). This transforms "regulations are tightening" into "developers need a solution by X date or face penalties."

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads as a feature roadmap, not a defensible technology. Add one concrete example showing how your AI actually works: for instance, "HelioHelp ingests building thermal load data, solar irradiance patterns, and water hardness levels to auto-generate collector array sizing — reducing design time from 3 weeks to 2 hours while accounting for GCC-specific overheating risks that generic tools miss." This shows investors the specific problem your AI solves and why a competitor can't easily replicate it.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.suggestion: You've built HelioHelp as your scaling engine, but investors will want to know what's under the hood — specifically, which cloud platforms, AI model providers, or third-party APIs power it. Add a brief technical dependencies section to Slide 19 that names your cloud infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP?), whether you're using proprietary AI or third-party models (OpenAI, custom-trained?), and any critical integrations, then note which ones you own versus license — this shows you've thought through vendor risk and aren't building on sand.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.suggestion: You're operating in a heavily regulated market—UAE mandates solar hot water, and you're deploying systems in hotels and utilities—but investors won't know your compliance posture unless you spell it out. Add one bullet to Slide 5 (Technology) under HelioPro stating something like "Compliant with UAE.S.S. 2050 solar thermal standards and hotel safety certifications" so buyers and investors immediately see you've engineered for the regulatory environment, not just around it.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides present financial projections with scenarios (Bear, Base, Bull) and unit economics components, but do NOT explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but lack detailed stated assumptions underlying the numbers.
findings.suggestion: Add a single "Assumptions" box to your financials slide that spells out the three drivers of your Base case: (1) customer acquisition—e.g., "50 units deployed by end 2026 via EtihadWE + Rixos, scaling to 200+ units/year by 2028 through ALDAR and Masdar partnerships," (2) hardware ASP and margin—e.g., "$15K–$20K per installation, 40% gross margin by 2027 as local manufacturing ramps," and (3) licensing uptake—e.g., "OEM licensing begins 2028 at $18K per partner, reaching

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and the Rixos payback period, but investors need to see CAC and LTV to understand how capital-efficient your customer acquisition really is—especially since you're scaling through developers and ESCOs. Add a simple box showing: "CAC: $X per installation (via developer partnerships)" and "LTV: $Y over 5-7 years (hardware + O&M contracts)" with the LTV:CAC ratio, so it's crystal clear that your partner-led model isn't burning cash to acquire customers.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy of $87,000 between the contracted amount and projected hardware revenue.
findings.suggestion: The $213K contracted amount on Slide 12 is your locked revenue, but Slide 14's $0.3M 2026 hardware projection includes additional pipeline beyond what's signed—clarify this by adding a footnote to the financial table that breaks out "Contracted: $213K" and "Pipeline/Projected: $87K" so investors see you're not double-counting and understand which revenue is de-risked versus dependent on closing the strategic discussions listed below.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated "Use of Funds" slide that breaks down exactly how you'll deploy this new capital over the next 18 months—for example: "Manufacturing tooling & inventory: $120K | Sales & operations hires: $100K | Pilot deployment & installation: $80K | Working capital: $50K" tied directly to hitting your 50-installation target and Q2 2026 revenue milestones. Right now investors see your $350K seed runway but have no visibility into whether this next round goes to scaling production, team, or something else entirely.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line under Rixos and EtihadWE stating the payment status explicitly — something like "Rixos: $X revenue received Q3 2024" or "EtihadWE: Contract signed, first payment received [date]" — so investors immediately see these aren't pilots but actual paying customers generating real cash. Right now it reads like a project list rather than proof of commercial traction.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: Right now your traction slide shows capacity and partnerships, but investors need to see actual revenue movement—add specific numbers like "Q3 revenue: $180K, Q4: $240K (+33% MoM)" or "Rixos project generated $85K in first three months, tracking to $120K by month 6." This proves your model works in practice, not just in theory.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: You've got a live customer in Rixos that's been running your system for over a year—that's gold, so show it. Add a single metric to your Rixos case study slide (Slide 4) that proves the customer is sticking around: something like "System uptime: 99.2% over 14 months" or "O&M contract renewed for 3 years" or even "Zero unplanned downtime incidents post-deployment." This turns a one-time win into evidence of reliability and customer satisfaction, which is exactly what investors want to see before they bet on your scaling model.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.suggestion: Name the actual competitors in your GCC market—right now you're comparing against product categories, not companies. Replace "Legacy Solar" with something like "Solargain's flat-mount systems" or "Thermex heat pumps" so investors immediately understand who you're taking share from and can validate your claims against real alternatives they know.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why you're better than existing solutions, but it doesn't explain why a major player like Bosch or Lennox couldn't just build this themselves tomorrow—add a slide that directly addresses this by laying out the specific barriers they'd face, whether that's your proprietary polymer absorber technology, your Gulf-climate testing data, your flat-roof installation partnerships, or regulatory certifications you already hold that would take them 18+ months to replicate. This transforms your competitive advantage from "we're better designed" into "we're defensible."

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Process Electrician Worker positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing the connective tissue that makes investors believe you can execute together—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members who fill gaps and validate your strategy. For example, add a line like "Advisory Board: [Name], former VP Manufacturing at [relevant solar/cleantech company], advising on supply chain scaling" or "Board Observer: [Name], led go-to-market for [comparable exit], guiding sales strategy"—this shows you're not just talented individuals, but a team with external validation and mentorship.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your Team slide stating the vesting schedule for key founders—something like "Dr. Faisal Ghani: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" next to his bio. This shows investors you've thought through retention incentives and prevents awkward questions during due diligence that could derail momentum.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated compliance slide before the market slide that maps SolarisKit against UAE solar thermal mandates (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel standards) and lists your current certifications—you mention Solar Keymark and ISO/EN certification in passing on slide 3, but investors in regulated markets need to see upfront that you've already cleared the compliance hurdles that kill competitors. For example: "Solar Keymark certified ✓ | Estidama-compliant design ✓ | Dubai Municipality approval pathway: Q2 2026 | Barjeel standards: met through hard-water polymer design" with a brief note on how each standard directly solves a failure mode

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You mention patented technology across three jurisdictions and trademark applications for HelioHelp, but investors will want to know if there are any existing patent challenges, freedom-to-operate issues, or disputes with competitors claiming overlapping IP—add a brief statement on Slide 8 (Defensible Technology) saying something like "No pending IP disputes or litigation; freedom-to-operate confirmed across target markets" so you're explicitly closing this question before it gets asked in the room.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these align. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' and the $400M SAM figure is unclear without additional context.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a single line showing how the 5% UAE market share ($20M) connects to your $400M SAM — for example: "5% UAE market share of $400M SAM = $20M annual revenue opportunity" or show the math explicitly so investors see the relationship isn't arbitrary. Right now it reads like two separate numbers, and that creates doubt about whether your market sizing is internally coherent.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, duplicate slide content appears in formatting
findings.suggestion: Go through the deck systematically and fix every instance: Slide 6 cuts off at "Strong push for dec" — complete it to "Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law)"; replace all [URL_REDACTED] placeholders with actual links or remove them entirely; and delete the duplicate slide content (you have the cover slide, Slide 4, Slide 6, Slide 7, Slide 11, Slide 15, and Slide 16 all appearing twice). A quick find-and-replace pass will catch these, and investors will immediately notice if your deck looks unfinished or has broken links.
